serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Mee Bandung as typically served at a hawker stall or coffee shop in Singapore or Johor weighs approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Bandung — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mee Bandung (cr_id F05060079). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from analogous yellow noodle dish composition using USDA FoodData Central reference (see notes).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; yellow wheat noodles (cooked) analogous reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Mee Bandung exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: cooked yellow wheat noodles (GI ~53–61, median 58) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 43.75) / 100 ≈ 25.4 for a 350 g plate; however, per-100g GL = (58 × 12.5) / 100 = 7.25, rounded to 7.3. Serving-based GL reported here as per-100g basis for consistency with nutrient table.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 428 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce and salt during cooking, as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,498 mg sodium — more than 65% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined yellow wheat noodles with medium GI (58) and low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g) producing a moderate glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit the noodle portion to approximately 150 g, increase the prawn and egg component to raise protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, and request wholemeal noodle substitution where available to blunt the post-meal glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (428 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (3.5 g/100 g) from frying oil, with limited unsaturated fat data
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request a smaller portion, ask for less oil during frying, and avoid adding extra chilli sauce, as the high sodium content combined with moderate fat from frying oil poses a cumulative cardiovascular risk over regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (428 mg/100 g), nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli), and refined carbohydrates driving systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint symptom flares from tomato and chilli (nightshades), request reduced chilli paste and soy sauce to lower sodium, and consider substituting refined yellow noodles with a higher-fibre alternative to reduce glycaemic and inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Bandung (万隆面) is a popular Malay noodle dish originating from Muar, Johor, Malaysia, and widely enjoyed in Singapore. It consists of yellow wheat noodles served in a rich, mildly spicy prawn-based gravy thickened with tomato and chilli sauce. Standard toppings include whole or halved prawns, a soft-boiled or fried egg, chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), sliced cucumber, green peas, onion, tomato, and shredded cabbage. The dish is typically assembled by blanching the noodles, preparing the gravy from prawn stock, tomato, chilli, and aromatics, then ladling the hot gravy over the noodles and garnishes. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet, and moderately spicy with a tangy tomato undertone. Texturally, the yellow noodles are soft and slightly chewy, contrasting with the crunchy cucumber and tender prawn. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 99 kcal, 4.3 g protein, 3.5 g fat, 12.5 g carbohydrate, 2.2 g sugar, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 428 mg sodium. The sodium content is clinically significant, exceeding 400 mg/100 g, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The medium glycaemic index (GI 58) and low fibre content require portion moderation for people with type 2 diabetes. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains gluten (wheat noodles), crustaceans (prawn), and egg allergens. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore and Johor.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mee bandung?
Is mee bandung suitable for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in mee bandung?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Mee Bandung (cr_id F05060079) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from analogous yellow noodle dish data in USDA FoodData Central. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (cooked yellow wheat noodles). All mandatory multilingual names present for a Singaporean/Malay dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked yellow wheat noodle dish (closest analogous food); not directly reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Malay composite noodle dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate based on cooked yellow wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mee Bandung exists. 4. Glycemic load per serving reported on a per-100g basis (7.3); full-plate GL for a 350 g serving is approximately 25.4. 5. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced 'red meat' in cautions — this appeared to be a copy-paste error as Mee Bandung does not contain red meat; this caution has been removed from the output. 6. Cultural dietary_tags included 'low_calorie' in the input; at 99 kcal/100g this is plausible for a noodle dish but borderline — retained as a cultural tag only, not a clinical claim.
